zones.name: CLI Command Layer
zones.description: Rex's CLI surface: command handlers for status, move, remove, verify, usage, and adapter management, plus shared output and error utilities.
insights: High coupling (0.6) — 30 imports target "prd-tree-operations"

Coupling at 0.6 hits the warning threshold — this zone imports from unit-core (30), unit-store (26), and unit-analyze (13), making it the most connected zone in the graph and a hotspot for ripple-effect changes.
cli receives imports from unit-core (10) and unit-store (7), meaning the dependency flows both directions; CLI modules should only be consumers, not providers, of domain logic.
token-format.ts and output.ts as entry points suggest formatting concerns are reused across commands — confirm these don't contain any business logic that belongs in the domain layer.
Coupling of 0.6 is at the warning threshold; this zone imports from three separate domain zones (unit-core, unit-store, unit-analyze), making it vulnerable to breakage from changes in any of them.
Reverse imports from unit-core and unit-store back into the cli zone violate the expected unidirectional dependency from CLI → domain; investigate whether domain modules have leaked CLI concerns.
Grouping shared CLI utilities (errors.ts, output.ts, validate-input.ts) in src/cli/ rather than scattering them across command files is a clean pattern that should be preserved.
Zone "cli-command-layer" has files across 6 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

src/core/move.ts imports CLIError from ../cli/errors.js — a domain module directly depending on a CLI type, confirming that CLI error types have leaked into domain logic rather than being defined in a shared foundation layer

CLIError defined in src/cli/errors.ts is imported by src/core/move.ts, making a domain module depend on a CLI module. This inverts the expected flow and should be resolved by moving CLIError to a shared errors or foundation layer.

The CLI zone functions as an unintended shared vocabulary layer: 30 reverse imports from unit-core (10), unit-store (7), and unit-analyze (13) all reach upward into CLI for utilities and types. This pattern indicates CLI-layer symbols have been promoted to cross-domain status without deliberate design.

cli-command-layer is the only production zone combining high coupling (0.6) and low cohesion (0.4) simultaneously — this combination is the canonical definition of a fragile zone: many inbound dependencies can break it, and its low internal cohesion means changes cascade unpredictably within it

chunked-review-state.ts (~870 lines) lives in src/cli/commands/ but its own header comment reads 'Pure logic (no I/O, fully testable)' — a domain-level interactive-review state machine with no CLI I/O is misplaced in the CLI commands directory; it belongs in src/analyze/ or a dedicated src/review/ module

token-format.ts mixes two unrelated concerns under one file name: pure formatting functions (formatAggregateTokenUsage, formatBudgetWarnings) and an IO-bound data-loading orchestration function (preflightBudgetCheck that resolves a store, reads config, and reads logs). The 'format' name only describes half the file's responsibility.

cli-command-layer is the only zone with BOTH high coupling (0.6) AND low cohesion (0.4) — the combination makes it the most fragile production zone in the codebase. Changes to any of three upstream zones (unit-core, unit-store, unit-analyze) can break it, while its low internal cohesion ensures failures propagate unpredictably. Splitting command files from shared CLI utilities (output.ts, errors.ts, validate-input.ts, token-format.ts) into separate zones would reduce both metrics.

chunked-review-state.ts is an ~870-line pure state machine explicitly documented as having 'no I/O' — move it out of src/cli/commands/ to src/analyze/ or src/review/ to match its domain role. Its current location inflates cli-command-layer's file count and hides a substantial domain concern inside the CLI layer.

token-format.ts should be split into two files: a pure formatting module (formatAggregateTokenUsage, formatBudgetWarnings) and a budget-check orchestration module (preflightBudgetCheck). Mixing IO-bound store access with presentation-only string formatting in a file named 'token-format' violates single responsibility and makes the file's scope invisible from its name.
[call graph] 835 internal calls, 90 outgoing, 400 incoming (cohesion: 0.9, coupling: 0.1)

zones.name: E2E Tests
zones.description: End-to-end CLI test suite covering adapter, analyze, import, prune, quiet mode, recommend, smart-add, sync, and related workflows against real CLI invocations.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling confirm a fully isolated test layer — no production code bleeds in, which is ideal
Coverage spans most major CLI commands (adapter, analyze, import, prune, recommend, smart-add, sync) — a healthy breadth signal for regression confidence
Zero coupling means these tests invoke the CLI as a black box; if internal behavior needs direct assertion, a separate integration layer already exists in tests/integration/

Perfect isolation (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0) confirms the e2e suite treats the CLI as a true black box — a strong architectural signal that the public CLI contract is stable and testable without internal access.
cli-recommend.test.ts and cli-smart-add.test.ts target the two most complex commands; ensuring these tests cover failure paths and edge inputs will protect the highest-risk user-facing flows.

The stark contrast between e2e-tests (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0) and unit-cli (cohesion 0.34) suggests the project's test strategy is bifurcated: end-to-end tests are disciplined and isolated while unit tests have accumulated without a consistent home; a zone-aligned unit test convention (co-locate test files with the zone they cover) would close this gap

The e2e zone's perfect cohesion (1.0) is structurally fragile: a single production import added to any test file breaks the isolation property with no automated guard to catch it. The invariant exists by convention only.

The e2e suite covers adapter, analyze, import, prune, quiet, recommend, smart-add, and sync commands but the file listing does not show coverage for the 'health' command (cli/commands/health.ts) or the 'fix' command (prd-store-sync) — both non-trivial commands with no apparent e2e test.

Add a dependency-cruiser or eslint-plugin-import rule that forbids e2e test files from importing any path outside tests/e2e/ or node built-ins. The current perfect isolation (coupling 0) is convention-only and will silently degrade as the test suite grows.

Add e2e tests for the 'health' and 'fix' CLI commands. These commands have production files in prd-tree-operations and prd-store-sync respectively but no corresponding e2e coverage visible in the zone, leaving two non-trivial user-facing flows unprotected at the integration boundary.
[call graph] 238 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

zones.name: PRD Analysis Pipeline
zones.description: The core PRD analysis engine: scanning projects, proposing items, deduplicating, diffing, extracting, and reshaping the PRD tree.
insights: 17 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
Cohesion of 0.66 is healthy for a 60-file zone — the community detection correctly grouped a large but coherent set of analysis concerns together.
The 17 entry points signal that this zone is a major API surface; auditing which ones are truly public vs. internal could reduce accidental coupling from other zones.

unit-analyze receives 13 imports from the cli zone and sends 13 back, indicating a bidirectional relationship that could introduce subtle coupling; verify there are no circular dependencies between these two zones.
Strong cohesion (0.66) across 60 files is a positive indicator that the Louvain community detection correctly identified a semantically unified analysis domain.

The bidirectional import relationship with the cli zone (13 imports each way) is a potential coupling risk; prefer making cli depend on unit-analyze unidirectionally to respect the four-tier hierarchy.

17 entry points for a single zone is high; introducing an explicit index barrel (src/analyze/index.ts already exists) and routing all external consumers through it would clarify the public API boundary.
Zone "prd-analysis-pipeline" has files across 7 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

src/analyze/guided.ts imports from ../cli/output.js, meaning analysis pipeline reaches into CLI for console output — this is a concrete example of the abstraction leak; the output concern should be injected or extracted to a shared utilities layer

unit-analyze participates in a three-zone dependency cycle: unit-core → unit-analyze (22 imports) → unit-store (37 imports) → unit-core (16 imports). This forms a closed loop across three zones and is the most critical structural risk in the codebase.

The single concrete reverse import (guided.ts → cli/output.js) is the root cause of the 13-import unit-analyze → cli relationship. Extracting a Logger or OutputPort interface that CLI implements would sever this inversion without restructuring files.

analyze/guided.ts embeds readline terminal I/O (the promptLine function reading from process.stdin) directly alongside AI clarification logic, mixing presentation concerns into a domain service and making the clarification round untestable without a real terminal.

guided.ts owns both the AI clarification protocol (buildClarifyPrompt, clarify) and interactive terminal I/O (promptLine using readline/process.stdin). Embedding process.stdin reading in a domain service makes unit testing impossible without mocking node internals and violates single responsibility — the prompting concern belongs in the CLI command that calls guided.ts.

The file naming convention within this zone splits into two distinct styles: verb-noun action files (extract.ts, dedupe.ts, diff.ts, decompose.ts, propose.ts) and noun/adjective-only files (guided.ts, reason.ts, scanners.ts, analyze-shared.ts). The action-verb pattern is more self-documenting; 'guided.ts' and 'reason.ts' in particular give no indication of what action they perform.

guided.ts and reason.ts follow a different naming convention than the rest of the zone (extract.ts, dedupe.ts, diff.ts use verb-noun). Rename to reflect action: guided.ts → clarify.ts or interactive-clarify.ts; reason.ts → build-reason.ts or reason-builder.ts. Consistent verb-noun naming across the zone makes the action each module performs visible from the filename alone.
[call graph] 2618 internal calls, 257 outgoing, 43 incoming (cohesion: 0.91, coupling: 0.09)

zones.name: PRD Store & Sync
zones.description: Data persistence layer responsible for schema definitions, canonical normalization, store contracts, and remote sync adapters (Jira, Notion).
insights: High coupling (0.53) — 16 imports target "prd-tree-operations"
18 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports

Houses both schema primitives (v1.ts, levels.ts, validate.ts) and sync infrastructure (sync-engine.ts, adapter-registry.ts) — consider splitting schema definitions from adapter plumbing if either grows independently
Integration schemas for Jira and Notion live under store/integration-schemas/ — a clean separation point if additional adapters are added
Bidirectional imports with unit-core (98 outbound, 16 inbound) suggest some coupling that may be hard to untangle; watch for emergent circular dependencies

16 imports flowing from unit-core back into unit-store create a bidirectional dependency loop; this is the primary architectural risk in the rex package and warrants an explicit audit of which core modules depend on store internals.

Cohesion of 0.47 is borderline — the zone conflates schema definition, canonical transformation, and remote sync adapters into a single community; these could be separated into storage-schema and sync-adapters sub-zones.
Zone "prd-store-sync" has files across 13 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

The 7 reverse imports from unit-store into unit-analyze suggest the store layer has begun pulling in analysis-pipeline logic — if analysis types or helpers have been copied into store modules to break a cycle, that duplication will drift over time

unit-store → unit-analyze (7 imports) combined with unit-analyze → unit-store (37 imports) forms a second bidirectional loop distinct from the unit-core ↔ unit-store loop; two simultaneous bidirectional cycles in the same zone elevate circular-dependency risk above what either cycle alone would imply

The zone conflates two distinct change-rate profiles: schema definitions (stable, infrequent changes) and sync adapter infrastructure (volatile, changes with each new integration). Files with different change rates co-located in the same zone inflate merge conflict probability and couple unrelated test suites to unrelated production changes.

Schema primitives (v1.ts, levels.ts, validate.ts) and remote sync adapters (sync-engine.ts, adapter-registry.ts, integration-schemas/) have mismatched change rates but share a zone. Schema changes should not trigger re-review of adapter code and vice versa — split into a schema-definitions sub-zone and a sync-adapters sub-zone.

The zone name 'prd-store-sync' emphasizes sync infrastructure but the zone also owns all schema definitions (v1.ts, levels.ts) — the name creates a false impression that the zone is exclusively sync plumbing, which leads new schema-related files to be placed in other zones rather than here.

45 files across 13 directories paired with a single-concern name ('store-sync') is a naming-architecture mismatch: the name should either be broadened to 'prd-schema-and-sync' or the zone split to match the implied scope of the current name.

Rename the zone to 'prd-schema-and-sync' or split it into 'prd-schema' (v1.ts, levels.ts, validate.ts — stable, infrequent changes) and 'prd-sync-adapters' (sync-engine.ts, adapter-registry.ts, integration-schemas/ — volatile). The current name under-describes scope and causes developers to underestimate the zone's breadth.
[call graph] 899 internal calls, 114 outgoing, 165 incoming (cohesion: 0.89, coupling: 0.11)

zones.name: PRD Tree Operations
zones.description: Core PRD runtime engine — tree traversal, DAG integrity, item lifecycle (move, prune, remove), facets, stats, and MCP tool dispatch.
insights: 21 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
Strongest cohesion (0.57) in the batch, reflecting a well-bounded domain of tree manipulation and query operations
MCP tool dispatch (mcp-tools.ts, mcp.ts) lives here alongside domain logic — as the MCP surface grows, consider extracting it to avoid mixing transport concerns with business logic
The 22 imports from unit-analyze confirm a natural layering: analysis produces proposals, tree-ops consume them; this directional flow is healthy
MCP tool handlers (mcp-tools.ts, mcp.ts) are co-located with core domain logic; if the MCP surface expands, a dedicated transport layer would keep this zone's cohesion high.
10 imports from unit-core back into the cli zone suggest some command-layer concerns have leaked into core; trace these to ensure no UI/formatting logic has settled in domain modules.
Zone "prd-tree-operations" has files across 7 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

At 64 files prd-tree-operations is 42 % larger than the next largest production zone; size alone does not indicate a problem, but combined with 21 entry points and co-located MCP transport it is the most likely zone to fragment further as the product grows

The three hub files (constants.ts, output.ts, tree.ts) are each imported by 5 zones and all three reside in or near prd-tree-operations; these files act as architectural glue that prevents clean zone boundaries — extracting them into a dedicated shared-primitives layer would reduce fan-in and let zone metrics reflect true domain cohesion

21 entry points across 64 files yields a 0.33 entry-points-per-file ratio — the widest implicit public API surface in the codebase. Without a barrel export enforcing what is actually public, every file in the zone is reachable by any consumer, making the coupling metrics an undercount of actual exposure.

mcp-tools.ts and mcp.ts co-located with domain logic creates a concrete maintenance hazard: any MCP protocol schema change (new required fields, renamed methods) forces edits to files that contain domain business logic, coupling transport evolution to domain stability.

mcp-tools.ts and mcp.ts are transport-layer files living inside the largest domain zone. A protocol change (e.g. MCP spec update) will require touching domain files, and a domain refactor risks breaking MCP serialization. Extract MCP dispatch to a dedicated transport zone.

21 public entry points with no enforcing barrel export means the zone has no defined public API — all 64 files are implicitly importable. This is the primary mechanism through which the 98-import unit-core→unit-store coupling accumulates: consumers import whatever internal file is convenient rather than a declared interface.

At 64 files with no enforcing barrel export, prd-tree-operations is the zone most at risk of absorbing unrelated files — any new file that imports tree.ts or output.ts will appear to 'belong' here by import proximity even if it has no tree-operation concern.

The zone name 'prd-tree-operations' accurately describes its primary concern but the presence of MCP transport files (mcp-tools.ts, mcp.ts) makes the name actively misleading: a developer searching for MCP dispatch code would not look in a zone named 'tree-operations'.

Relocate mcp-tools.ts and mcp.ts out of prd-tree-operations into a dedicated transport zone or directory (e.g. src/transport/ or src/mcp/). The zone name 'prd-tree-operations' signals domain logic, not protocol dispatch — co-location actively misleads developers searching for transport code.

Create a src/shared/ or src/primitives/ directory and move constants.ts, output.ts, and tree.ts there. These three files are imported by 5 zones each but live in cli/commands/ or core/ — their physical location implies zone membership they do not have, making every consumer appear as a cross-zone import when these are actually foundation-layer accesses.
[call graph] 2253 internal calls, 139 outgoing, 106 incoming (cohesion: 0.94, coupling: 0.06)

zones.name: PRD Validation
zones.description: Structural integrity checks for the PRD — validate commands, epic-correlation heuristics, and interactive fix prompts — co-located with their unit and integration tests.
insights: Low cohesion (0.34) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone
High coupling (0.66) — 19 imports target "prd-store-sync"
Low cohesion (0.34) and high coupling (0.66) are the weakest metrics in this batch; epic-correlation.ts and structural.ts are likely pulled into many consumers, inflating coupling

The zone mixes production files (validate.ts, validate-interactive.ts, epic-correlation.ts, structural.ts) with tests — the test files likely follow the production modules by import proximity rather than intent
Extracting epic-correlation.ts and structural.ts into prd-tree-operations or a dedicated analysis-support module could reduce coupling and raise cohesion

Cohesion of 0.34 and coupling of 0.66 exceed warning thresholds; epic-correlation and structural utilities appear to be widely imported utilities that have not yet found a stable home in the zone hierarchy.

19 imports from this zone into unit-store and 7 into unit-core indicate validation logic reaches across both major layers; consider whether structural checks belong in the store layer or as a validation pass in unit-core.
Zone "prd-validation" has files across 5 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Because prd-validation co-mingles test files with production files, its coupling metric of 0.66 is partially inflated by test-to-production imports; the true production coupling is unknowable without separating the two, making the metric misleading for architectural decisions

Test files in prd-validation (validate.test.ts, validate-interactive.test.ts, etc.) share a community with production files solely because of import proximity; this contaminates cohesion and coupling scores and should be resolved by moving tests to the unit zone or co-locating them with the unit-cli zone rather than letting them drift into a production-dominated zone

With only 4 production files, prd-validation is too small to justify independent zone status. epic-correlation.ts and structural.ts are domain utilities consumed across prd-tree-operations; validate.ts and validate-interactive.ts are CLI commands. Dissolving this zone into its natural hosts would eliminate the zone's low cohesion and high coupling without any logic changes.

prd-validation contains 4 production files that belong to two different architectural layers: epic-correlation.ts and structural.ts are domain utilities (should join prd-tree-operations) while validate.ts and validate-interactive.ts are CLI commands (should join cli). The zone exists as an artifact of import proximity, not architectural intent.

prd-validation is the only zone that simultaneously fails both the cohesion threshold (0.34 < 0.4) and the coupling threshold (0.66 > 0.6) — it is the sole zone in a doubly-failed state, making it the highest-priority dissolution target in the codebase.

The zone name 'prd-validation' implies a domain layer but validate.ts and validate-interactive.ts are CLI commands — the name masks the architectural category of half its production files and will cause future CLI commands with 'validate' in their name to be incorrectly placed here.

Dissolve prd-validation as a top-priority action: move epic-correlation.ts and structural.ts into prd-tree-operations (domain utilities); move validate.ts and validate-interactive.ts into the cli zone (CLI commands). This is a zero-logic-change refactor that eliminates the only doubly-failed zone in the codebase.

Zone name 'prd-validation' is a misnomer for its actual contents. Even before dissolving, renaming would prevent future misassignment of CLI commands into this zone based on the misleading 'validation' label.
[call graph] 466 internal calls, 74 outgoing, 3 incoming (cohesion: 0.86, coupling: 0.14)

zones.name: Project Root
zones.description: Top-level project configuration, documentation, and build metadata that anchors the monorepo.
insights: Isolated files — no import edges between 9 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
context.md and llms.txt at the root act as AI-readable entry points for the whole repo — keeping them current as the codebase evolves is important for tool accuracy.
No cross-zone imports from root is correct: config files should never import from source packages.
package-lock.json coexisting with pnpm suggests a mixed lockfile situation worth auditing to avoid install inconsistencies.
Zero cohesion and coupling are expected for a root config zone; these files have no import graph participation.
Both package-lock.json and pnpm are present; confirm lockfile discipline is enforced (e.g. .npmrc or CI checks) to prevent accidental npm installs from diverging from pnpm state.

context.md and llms.txt exist at the project root alongside the canonical .sourcevision/CONTEXT.md and .sourcevision/llms.txt. The root-level copies are likely stale and will silently diverge from the generated sourcevision output; AI tools indexing the repo root may consume outdated context without realizing it.

Root-level context.md and llms.txt shadow the canonical .sourcevision/CONTEXT.md and .sourcevision/llms.txt. If the root copies are not auto-generated by the build pipeline, they will silently go stale. Either delete them (and update any AI tool configuration pointing at the root path) or add a CI check that verifies the root copies are symlinks or exact copies of the generated sourcevision output.

zones.name: Recommend & Acknowledge
zones.description: The recommendation workflow: accepting sourcevision-driven PRD proposals, detecting conflicts, and wiring the recommend CLI command.
insights: High coupling (0.57) — 9 imports target "prd-store-sync"
Coupling at 0.57 is approaching the warning threshold — this zone imports broadly from unit-store (9 imports) and unit-core (4 imports), meaning it depends on two other major zones.
Test files are co-located with production source in the same zone, which is fine — the '-tests' suffix is correctly avoided since production entry points are present.
create-from-recommendations.ts is a key entry point; ensuring it has a single, clear responsibility (transform recommendations → PRD mutations) will keep this zone from accumulating mixed concerns.

Coupling of 0.57 is near the warning boundary; the zone reaches into both unit-store and unit-core, suggesting recommend logic has broad dependencies that could be reduced by narrowing what it imports from the store layer.
conflict-detection.ts is isolated enough to be a candidate for extraction into unit-core if conflict logic proves reusable across other analyze workflows.
Zone "recommend-acknowledge" has files across 6 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

The recommend-acknowledge zone's outbound dependencies (unit-store: 9, unit-core: 4) flow strictly downward in the intended hierarchy — recommend → store → core — making it one of the few zones with purely unidirectional cross-zone dependencies

recommend-acknowledge is the only multi-file production zone with no reverse imports flowing back into it from its dependencies, making it a clean consumer zone with a well-defined dependency direction.

create-from-recommendations.ts re-exports shared types 'for backwards compatibility' (lines 29–33), a pattern the project guidelines explicitly prohibit — downstream consumers should import from ./types.js directly rather than relying on the shim.

create-from-recommendations.ts re-exports EnrichedRecommendation, RecommendationMeta, and ConflictStrategy from ./types.js solely for backwards compatibility. The project's own CLAUDE.md forbids backwards-compatibility shims; these re-exports should be removed and callers updated to import from the canonical source.

The zone name 'recommend-acknowledge' describes only two of the zone's three production concerns — conflict-detection.ts is a third distinct concern not reflected in the zone name. As the zone grows, this naming gap will make it harder to reason about zone membership from the name alone.

Zone name 'recommend-acknowledge' omits conflict-detection.ts, which implements a distinct third concern. Consider renaming to 'recommend-workflow' or extracting conflict-detection into a separate zone as it grows — the current name will mislead developers trying to locate conflict logic from zone metadata.
[call graph] 174 internal calls, 47 outgoing, 4 incoming (cohesion: 0.79, coupling: 0.21)

zones.name: Rex Runtime State
zones.description: Persistent runtime artifacts for the rex PRD manager: project config, the PRD tree, workflow state, and execution logs.
insights: Isolated files — no import edges between 4 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
These are data files with no import relationships, so zero cohesion/coupling is expected and correct — treat them as a storage layer, not a code zone.
Separating config.json from prd.json keeps project-level settings decoupled from PRD content, which is a clean data boundary.
execution-log.jsonl growing unbounded could become a maintenance concern; a rotation or truncation policy would be worth adding.
Zero cohesion and coupling are expected: this zone contains only data/config files with no TypeScript imports between them.
workflow.md and execution-log.jsonl serve as human-readable audit trails alongside the machine-readable prd.json, providing good observability into rex state.
Hub: src/cli/commands/constants.ts is imported by 5 zones — cross-cutting dependency
Hub: src/cli/output.ts is imported by 5 zones — cross-cutting dependency
Hub: src/core/tree.ts is imported by 5 zones — cross-cutting dependency
Bidirectional coupling: "prd-store-sync" ↔ "prd-tree-operations" (16+98 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "prd-analysis-pipeline" ↔ "prd-store-sync" (37+7 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "cli-command-layer" ↔ "prd-tree-operations" (30+10 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "prd-analysis-pipeline" ↔ "prd-tree-operations" (13+22 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "cli-command-layer" ↔ "prd-store-sync" (26+7 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "cli-command-layer" ↔ "prd-analysis-pipeline" (4+13 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "prd-store-sync" ↔ "recommend-acknowledge" (1+9 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "prd-tree-operations" ↔ "prd-validation" (2+7 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "prd-tree-operations" ↔ "recommend-acknowledge" (4+4 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "prd-analysis-pipeline" ↔ "recommend-acknowledge" (2+2 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "cli-command-layer" ↔ "prd-validation" (2+2 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "cli-command-layer" ↔ "recommend-acknowledge" (1+1 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Size imbalance: largest zone has 64 files vs smallest with 4 — uneven decomposition

The unit-core ↔ unit-store bidirectional relationship (98 + 16 imports) is the heaviest coupling in the graph; confirm this is intentional layering and not a sign of circular dependencies between core domain logic and the store.

The cli zone sits at the top of the import fan-in graph but also has reverse imports flowing back into it from domain zones — this asymmetry suggests CLI utilities or types have leaked into domain logic and should be audited.
The e2e zone (cohesion: 1.0) is a standout: perfect internal cohesion means end-to-end tests are well-isolated from production code, which is the ideal test zone structure.

unit-cli (cohesion: 0.34, 11 files) has the lowest cohesion of all zones — it may be a catch-all for CLI tests that belong closer to their respective command zones; consider whether co-locating tests with their commands (as unit-analyze does) would improve cohesion.

The dominant cross-zone flow (unit-core → unit-store: 98 imports) reflects correct layering — tree operations depend on storage — but the 16 reverse imports (unit-store → unit-core) break that directionality and should be audited for circular risk

unit-analyze imports from both unit-store (37) and unit-core (13) while also being imported by unit-core (22), creating a triangle; if analysis results are consumed by core to mutate the tree, this is expected, but the cycle should be documented as an intentional design decision

The CLI Command Layer zone (24 files, cohesion 0.4) and unit-core both contain CLI command files — the split appears to be functional (query/navigation commands vs. mutation commands) but is not obvious from names alone; explicit documentation of which commands live where would reduce onboarding friction
e2e-tests stands entirely apart from all production zones (coupling 0), which validates that the CLI binary boundary is clean and no test-only abstractions have leaked into production modules

Cross-zone import density is highest between unit-core and unit-store (114 total bidirectional imports), suggesting these two zones are tightly coupled and may benefit from explicit interface contracts or a shared types boundary.

The four-tier hierarchy (orchestration → execution → domain → foundation) is reflected in the zone graph, but the cli zone's reverse imports from domain zones indicate partial violations worth resolving.
Data zones (.rex, root) correctly show zero cohesion/coupling since they hold only configuration and documentation — the community detection appropriately isolated them from the code graph.

Bidirectional imports between unit-store and unit-core (98 + 16) are the primary coupling risk in the codebase; a dependency inversion — having store expose an interface that core implements — could break the cycle without restructuring file locations.

unit-analyze imports from cli (13 imports) which itself imports from unit-core and unit-store — analysis pipeline reaching into the CLI layer is an inversion of the expected dependency direction and should be replaced with shared constants or types.

Five of six zones have healthy cohesion (≥0.43) and the e2e zone achieves perfect isolation, indicating the overall zone structure reflects genuine architectural intent rather than arbitrary file grouping.

src/cli/commands/ contains files from 6 zones (cli-command-layer, prd-analysis-pipeline, prd-store-sync, prd-tree-operations, prd-validation, recommend-acknowledge) — consider grouping into subdirectories to clarify architectural boundaries

The three-zone cycle unit-core → unit-analyze → unit-store → unit-core (22+37+16 imports) is the most structurally critical issue in the codebase. No zone in this triangle can be safely refactored without risk of breaking the others.

Two symbols — CLIError (cli/errors.ts) and output utilities (cli/output.ts) — are responsible for nearly all upward imports from domain zones into the CLI layer. Extracting these into a foundation-level @n-dx/errors or shared utilities package would eliminate the bidirectional cli↔domain coupling without restructuring zone membership.

Of all zone pairs, only recommend-acknowledge → unit-store and e2e → (nothing) represent fully unidirectional, non-cyclic relationships. Every other major zone pair is bidirectional, indicating the codebase lacks enforced layering boundaries.

The store barrel (src/store/index.ts) re-exporting core symbols means the store public API surface is larger than the store zone itself — consumers of the store receive hidden transitive access to core internals, undermining the store/core boundary.

src/cli/commands/constants.ts and src/cli/output.ts being imported by 5 zones each confirms these two files are de facto foundation-layer modules living in the wrong directory; relocating them to a shared constants or foundation package would reflect their actual architectural role.

The unit-analyze → cli import edge (13 imports) is the most structurally inverted relationship in the graph: the analysis pipeline (a domain layer) importing from the CLI layer (presentation) reverses the expected dependency direction; these 13 imports almost certainly pull in shared constants or output helpers that should instead live in a foundation module

The three hub files (src/cli/commands/constants.ts, src/cli/output.ts, src/core/tree.ts) collectively bridge 5 zones each; their presence in two different directories (cli/commands and core) means there is no single place to look for shared primitives, which makes the dependency surface harder to audit and increases the risk of accidental coupling as new files are added

The unit-core → unit-store flow (98 imports) dominates the entire cross-zone graph; all other flows are an order of magnitude smaller, which means architectural decisions about this single edge have outsized impact on overall coupling health — it warrants an explicit documented interface contract even if the directionality is intentional

prd-store-sync spans 13 directories while prd-tree-operations spans 7; the directory spread of prd-store-sync is the highest in the codebase and means its zone boundary is invisible to a developer navigating the file tree — adding an explicit architectural decision record (ADR) or README identifying which directories belong to this zone would reduce onboarding friction without any code changes

The three-zone dependency cycle (unit-core ↔ unit-analyze ↔ unit-store) means there is no valid topological sort of these three zones — they cannot be independently built, tested, or refactored. Introducing explicit interface boundaries (e.g. an AnalysisPort that core defines and analyze implements) is required to break the cycle.

CLI-layer symbols (CLIError, output helpers, constants) imported by domain zones are the single root cause of most bidirectional coupling in the graph. A targeted extraction of these symbols to a foundation layer would resolve multiple warning- and critical-severity issues simultaneously.

The store barrel re-exporting core symbols and the core zone importing from CLI form a two-sided encapsulation failure: the store leaks core internals upward while core imports CLI concerns downward. Together these erode both the store/core and core/cli boundaries.

Two distinct bidirectional cycles exist simultaneously: unit-core ↔ unit-store (98+16) and unit-analyze ↔ unit-store (37+7); cycles that share a common node (unit-store) can mask transitive circular dependencies that are not visible in pairwise analysis — a full cycle check (e.g. madge --circular) should be run to confirm no hidden three-node cycles exist

Hub files imported by 5+ zones (constants.ts, output.ts, tree.ts) are scattered across cli/commands/ and core/ with no dedicated shared-primitives package or directory; as the codebase grows, ad-hoc imports of these files will continue to inflate coupling scores across every zone that needs them

The clean boundary between e2e-tests and all production zones (coupling 0) proves the CLI binary surface is stable; this boundary should be treated as a contract and protected by a lint rule preventing any production module from importing test utilities, to avoid the boundary eroding as the test suite grows

public.ts:362 exports createRexMcpServer from ./cli/mcp.js as part of the package's primary public API. This couples every programmatic consumer of the rex library (e.g. hench, web) to the MCP transport layer, even those that only need domain functions. An MCP server factory is infrastructure, not domain, and should be exported via a separate subpath (e.g. rex/mcp) rather than the main entry point.

store/index.ts:6 re-exports SyncEngine from ../core/sync-engine.js through the store barrel. SyncEngine orchestrates bidirectional sync between two PRDStore instances — it is a core domain service, not a store adapter. Its re-export through the store barrel is the concrete mechanism causing the known 'store barrel leaks core internals' anti-pattern; moving SyncEngine out of the store barrel would directly reduce the unit-store → unit-core reverse import count.

core/move.ts:164 throws CLIError imported from ../cli/errors.js inside the domain function moveItem. Any programmatic caller of moveItem (e.g. hench via the rex-gateway) must either catch a CLI-layer error type or ignore the error detail, creating an invisible cross-layer dependency at the call site that is not visible from the import graph of the caller.

unit-analyze occupies the most structurally contradictory position in the import graph: it imports from cli (13), unit-store (37), and unit-core (13) while simultaneously being imported by unit-core (22). A zone that is depended upon by unit-core should not itself depend on unit-core — this is a direct indicator of a hidden circular dependency path that pairwise analysis cannot reveal but a full cycle check (madge --circular) would surface.

No import-boundary enforcement tooling (eslint-plugin-import, dependency-cruiser, or similar) is configured. Every architectural constraint identified in these zone reports is documentation-only. Without automated enforcement, the coupling issues will worsen monotonically as new files are added — the bidirectional cycles in particular cannot be held stable by convention alone.

The unit-analyze → cli import edge (13 imports) combined with cli → unit-core (30) and cli → unit-store (26) creates an implicit transitive path: unit-analyze → cli → unit-core/unit-store. This means unit-analyze has two dependency paths to unit-core (direct: 13 imports; transitive through cli) and two paths to unit-store (direct: 37; transitive through cli), making refactoring of any single edge insufficient to break the coupling.

The three hub files (constants.ts, output.ts, tree.ts) are each imported by 5 zones but live in two different directories (cli/commands/ and core/). Their physical separation means there is no single directory a developer can audit to understand the full shared-primitives surface — new files placed in either directory may inadvertently become de facto hubs before the pattern is recognized.

public.ts exports createRexMcpServer (an MCP/HTTP transport factory) alongside pure domain functions like findNextTask and resolveStore. Infrastructure and domain are mixed in the same entry point, forcing all consumers to transitively depend on the MCP server implementation. A subpath export ("./mcp") would isolate this without breaking existing callers.

store/index.ts re-exports SyncEngine from ../core/sync-engine.js. SyncEngine has no store-layer responsibility — it coordinates two PRDStore instances using core sync primitives. This re-export is the concrete cause of the known store-barrel-leaks-core-internals anti-pattern and should be removed from the store barrel; consumers can import SyncEngine directly from the core subpath or via public.ts.

core/move.ts imports and throws CLIError from ../cli/errors.js in the domain function moveItem. Domain functions should throw domain errors (e.g. a ValidationError defined in schema or core) rather than CLI-layer error types. This forces programmatic consumers of moveItem to import from the CLI layer to distinguish this error class, invisibly propagating the cli→domain coupling inversion beyond what the import graph reveals.

No import enforcement tooling is present in the repository. All architectural layer rules (domain must not import CLI, store must not import analyze, etc.) are enforced only by code review. Add dependency-cruiser or eslint-plugin-import with explicit forbidden-imports rules mirroring the four-tier hierarchy to make violations fail CI.

unit-analyze imports from the cli zone (13 imports) while unit-core imports from unit-analyze (22 imports), creating a transitive path unit-core → unit-analyze → cli that inverts the four-tier hierarchy. Core domain logic has indirect access to CLI presentation concerns through this chain. Eliminating the unit-analyze → cli imports is the highest-leverage single change to restore correct layering.

Zone naming uses two incompatible taxonomies across the codebase: some zones follow a domain-concern pattern (prd-analysis-pipeline, prd-store-sync, recommend-acknowledge) while others use a layer-type pattern (cli-command-layer). This inconsistency makes zone names non-comparable — a developer cannot infer from zone names alone whether a zone is named by its domain or by its architectural layer.

chunked-review-state.ts contains the single largest block of pure domain logic (~870 lines, explicitly documented as 'no I/O') in the CLI commands directory. Its misplacement inflates cli-command-layer's file count and hides the review-session state machine from zone tooling that correctly classifies domain logic — this one file move would meaningfully shift both cli-command-layer's cohesion score and its file count.

The three files with the broadest cross-zone reach (constants.ts, output.ts, tree.ts — each imported by 5 zones) have no shared naming convention signaling their hub status. A developer adding a new file has no signal that these files are architectural hubs requiring extra caution before modification. A naming convention (e.g. a *.shared.ts or *.hub.ts suffix) or a dedicated src/shared/ directory would make hub status explicit.

Zone naming conventions are inconsistent across the entire codebase: production zones use 'prd-*' (prd-store-sync, prd-tree-operations, prd-validation), test zones use 'e2e-tests' and 'unit-*', and some zones have no prefix at all (cli, unit, root). The mixed convention makes zone architectural category ambiguous at a glance and is especially confusing for the 'cli' zone which sits alongside 'prd-*' peers despite being a different architectural layer.

The three 'prd-*' zones have radically different sizes (11, 45, 64 files) despite sharing a naming family that implies peer-level zones. The size disparity (nearly 6:1 between smallest and largest) signals that the 'prd-*' family is not a coherent decomposition — the prefix grouping is misleading rather than clarifying.

Across all zones, only e2e-tests and (partially) recommend-acknowledge have unidirectional cross-zone relationships. Every major production zone pair is bidirectional, meaning the entire production zone graph has no valid topological ordering — not just the known three-node cycle. This extends the circular-dependency risk beyond unit-core/unit-analyze/unit-store to the full graph.

The Louvain community detection produced a zone (prd-validation) with only 4 production files — well below the minimum viable size for independent architectural analysis or meaningful cohesion/coupling metrics. A minimum zone size threshold (~8 production files) should be applied in future analysis runs to auto-dissolve micro-zones into adjacent communities rather than reporting them as independent architectural units.

No production zone in the codebase simultaneously achieves cohesion > 0.7 and coupling < 0.3 — the target quadrant for a well-bounded domain module. e2e-tests achieves perfect isolation but is a test zone. Establishing even one production zone as a reference architecture (the schema-definitions sub-zone extracted from prd-store-sync is the most achievable candidate given its stable change rate and no logic dependencies) would give the team a concrete model to replicate when splitting other zones.

Adopt a single zone naming convention: either all zones named by domain concern (prd-*, recommend-*) or all zones named by layer (cli-*, store-*, core-*). The current mixture of both taxonomies makes zone IDs non-comparable and forces developers to inspect zone contents to understand naming rationale.

The three hub files (constants.ts, output.ts, tree.ts) imported by 5 zones each have no naming or directory signal indicating their hub status. Before any of these files is modified or new symbols are added, there is no way to know from the filename alone that the change will ripple across 5 zones. Adding a .shared suffix or consolidating into a src/shared/ directory would make hub risk visible at the file-system level.

Root-level context.md and llms.txt at the project root coexist with .sourcevision/CONTEXT.md and .sourcevision/llms.txt — two sets of AI-readable context files with no enforcement that they stay in sync. Add a CI step that either removes the root copies or verifies they match the sourcevision-generated output to prevent AI tools from consuming stale context.

Establish a zone naming convention in CLAUDE.md or an ADR: e.g. 'src-*' for production domain zones, 'cli-*' for CLI-layer zones, 'test-*' for test zones, 'data-*' for config/data zones. Current mixed naming (prd-*, unit-*, e2e-tests, cli, root) makes architectural category ambiguous and will worsen as new zones are created.

Run `madge --circular packages/rex/src` to enumerate all circular dependency paths before the next architectural refactor. Known bidirectional zone pairs are pairwise observations only — three-node or longer cycles (e.g. unit-core → unit-analyze → cli → unit-core via transitive imports) cannot be detected from zone metrics and may already exist.

No production zone achieves the target quadrant (cohesion > 0.7, coupling < 0.3). Prioritize extracting a pure 'schema-definitions' zone (v1.ts, levels.ts from prd-store-sync) as a concrete architectural reference point — it is the only candidate with a stable change rate, no logic dependencies, and a natural extraction boundary.

The three hub files (constants.ts, output.ts, tree.ts), each imported by 5 zones, are scattered across two directories (cli/commands/ and core/). There is no single location a developer can audit to understand the full shared-primitives surface. A dedicated src/shared/ or src/primitives/ directory containing only these foundation-level files would make the surface explicit and prevent ad-hoc additions from becoming undiscovered hubs.
[call graph] 200 calls: "prd-analysis-pipeline" → "cli-command-layer"
[call graph] 75 calls: "prd-store-sync" → "cli-command-layer"
[call graph] 73 calls: "prd-tree-operations" → "prd-store-sync"
[call graph] 54 calls: "cli-command-layer" → "prd-store-sync"
[call graph] 48 calls: "prd-tree-operations" → "cli-command-layer"

findings.text: High coupling (0.6) — 30 imports target "prd-tree-operations"
Coupling of 0.6 is at the warning threshold; this zone imports from three separate domain zones (unit-core, unit-store, unit-analyze), making it vulnerable to breakage from changes in any of them.
Grouping shared CLI utilities (errors.ts, output.ts, validate-input.ts) in src/cli/ rather than scattering them across command files is a clean pattern that should be preserved.
Reverse imports from unit-core and unit-store back into the cli zone violate the expected unidirectional dependency from CLI → domain; investigate whether domain modules have leaked CLI concerns.
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

Perfect isolation (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0) confirms the e2e suite treats the CLI as a true black box — a strong architectural signal that the public CLI contract is stable and testable without internal access.
cli-recommend.test.ts and cli-smart-add.test.ts target the two most complex commands; ensuring these tests cover failure paths and edge inputs will protect the highest-risk user-facing flows.
Bidirectional coupling: "prd-store-sync" ↔ "prd-tree-operations" (16+98 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Hub: src/cli/commands/constants.ts is imported by 5 zones — cross-cutting dependency
Size imbalance: largest zone has 64 files vs smallest with 4 — uneven decomposition
Fan-in hotspot: src/schema/index.ts receives calls from 23 files — high-impact module, changes may have wide ripple effects
17 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports

17 entry points for a single zone is high; introducing an explicit index barrel (src/analyze/index.ts already exists) and routing all external consumers through it would clarify the public API boundary.
Strong cohesion (0.66) across 60 files is a positive indicator that the Louvain community detection correctly identified a semantically unified analysis domain.

The bidirectional import relationship with the cli zone (13 imports each way) is a potential coupling risk; prefer making cli depend on unit-analyze unidirectionally to respect the four-tier hierarchy.
18 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
High coupling (0.53) — 16 imports target "prd-tree-operations"

16 imports flowing from unit-core back into unit-store create a bidirectional dependency loop; this is the primary architectural risk in the rex package and warrants an explicit audit of which core modules depend on store internals.

Cohesion of 0.47 is borderline — the zone conflates schema definition, canonical transformation, and remote sync adapters into a single community; these could be separated into storage-schema and sync-adapters sub-zones.
21 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
10 imports from unit-core back into the cli zone suggest some command-layer concerns have leaked into core; trace these to ensure no UI/formatting logic has settled in domain modules.
MCP tool handlers (mcp-tools.ts, mcp.ts) are co-located with core domain logic; if the MCP surface expands, a dedicated transport layer would keep this zone's cohesion high.
High coupling (0.66) — 19 imports target "prd-store-sync"
Low cohesion (0.34) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone

19 imports from this zone into unit-store and 7 into unit-core indicate validation logic reaches across both major layers; consider whether structural checks belong in the store layer or as a validation pass in unit-core.

Cohesion of 0.34 and coupling of 0.66 exceed warning thresholds; epic-correlation and structural utilities appear to be widely imported utilities that have not yet found a stable home in the zone hierarchy.
Isolated files — no import edges between 9 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
Both package-lock.json and pnpm are present; confirm lockfile discipline is enforced (e.g. .npmrc or CI checks) to prevent accidental npm installs from diverging from pnpm state.
Zero cohesion and coupling are expected for a root config zone; these files have no import graph participation.
High coupling (0.57) — 9 imports target "prd-store-sync"

Coupling of 0.57 is near the warning boundary; the zone reaches into both unit-store and unit-core, suggesting recommend logic has broad dependencies that could be reduced by narrowing what it imports from the store layer.
conflict-detection.ts is isolated enough to be a candidate for extraction into unit-core if conflict logic proves reusable across other analyze workflows.
Isolated files — no import edges between 4 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
Zero cohesion and coupling are expected: this zone contains only data/config files with no TypeScript imports between them.
workflow.md and execution-log.jsonl serve as human-readable audit trails alongside the machine-readable prd.json, providing good observability into rex state.
Zone "cli-command-layer" has files across 6 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

chunked-review-state.ts is an ~870-line pure state machine explicitly documented as having 'no I/O' — move it out of src/cli/commands/ to src/analyze/ or src/review/ to match its domain role. Its current location inflates cli-command-layer's file count and hides a substantial domain concern inside the CLI layer.

cli-command-layer is the only zone with BOTH high coupling (0.6) AND low cohesion (0.4) — the combination makes it the most fragile production zone in the codebase. Changes to any of three upstream zones (unit-core, unit-store, unit-analyze) can break it, while its low internal cohesion ensures failures propagate unpredictably. Splitting command files from shared CLI utilities (output.ts, errors.ts, validate-input.ts, token-format.ts) into separate zones would reduce both metrics.

token-format.ts should be split into two files: a pure formatting module (formatAggregateTokenUsage, formatBudgetWarnings) and a budget-check orchestration module (preflightBudgetCheck). Mixing IO-bound store access with presentation-only string formatting in a file named 'token-format' violates single responsibility and makes the file's scope invisible from its name.

src/cli/commands/ contains files from 6 zones (cli-command-layer, prd-analysis-pipeline, prd-store-sync, prd-tree-operations, prd-validation, recommend-acknowledge) — consider grouping into subdirectories to clarify architectural boundaries

Adopt a single zone naming convention: either all zones named by domain concern (prd-*, recommend-*) or all zones named by layer (cli-*, store-*, core-*). The current mixture of both taxonomies makes zone IDs non-comparable and forces developers to inspect zone contents to understand naming rationale.

Establish a zone naming convention in CLAUDE.md or an ADR: e.g. 'src-*' for production domain zones, 'cli-*' for CLI-layer zones, 'test-*' for test zones, 'data-*' for config/data zones. Current mixed naming (prd-*, unit-*, e2e-tests, cli, root) makes architectural category ambiguous and will worsen as new zones are created.

No production zone achieves the target quadrant (cohesion > 0.7, coupling < 0.3). Prioritize extracting a pure 'schema-definitions' zone (v1.ts, levels.ts from prd-store-sync) as a concrete architectural reference point — it is the only candidate with a stable change rate, no logic dependencies, and a natural extraction boundary.

Root-level context.md and llms.txt at the project root coexist with .sourcevision/CONTEXT.md and .sourcevision/llms.txt — two sets of AI-readable context files with no enforcement that they stay in sync. Add a CI step that either removes the root copies or verifies they match the sourcevision-generated output to prevent AI tools from consuming stale context.

Run `madge --circular packages/rex/src` to enumerate all circular dependency paths before the next architectural refactor. Known bidirectional zone pairs are pairwise observations only — three-node or longer cycles (e.g. unit-core → unit-analyze → cli → unit-core via transitive imports) cannot be detected from zone metrics and may already exist.

The three hub files (constants.ts, output.ts, tree.ts) imported by 5 zones each have no naming or directory signal indicating their hub status. Before any of these files is modified or new symbols are added, there is no way to know from the filename alone that the change will ripple across 5 zones. Adding a .shared suffix or consolidating into a src/shared/ directory would make hub risk visible at the file-system level.

The three hub files (constants.ts, output.ts, tree.ts), each imported by 5 zones, are scattered across two directories (cli/commands/ and core/). There is no single location a developer can audit to understand the full shared-primitives surface. A dedicated src/shared/ or src/primitives/ directory containing only these foundation-level files would make the surface explicit and prevent ad-hoc additions from becoming undiscovered hubs.
Hub function: walkTree in src/core/tree.ts is called from 29 files (high fan-in expected for this module type) — changes here have wide impact, consider if responsibilities can be narrowed
Zone "prd-analysis-pipeline" has files across 7 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

guided.ts and reason.ts follow a different naming convention than the rest of the zone (extract.ts, dedupe.ts, diff.ts use verb-noun). Rename to reflect action: guided.ts → clarify.ts or interactive-clarify.ts; reason.ts → build-reason.ts or reason-builder.ts. Consistent verb-noun naming across the zone makes the action each module performs visible from the filename alone.
Zone "prd-store-sync" has files across 13 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Rename the zone to 'prd-schema-and-sync' or split it into 'prd-schema' (v1.ts, levels.ts, validate.ts — stable, infrequent changes) and 'prd-sync-adapters' (sync-engine.ts, adapter-registry.ts, integration-schemas/ — volatile). The current name under-describes scope and causes developers to underestimate the zone's breadth.
Zone "prd-tree-operations" has files across 7 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Create a src/shared/ or src/primitives/ directory and move constants.ts, output.ts, and tree.ts there. These three files are imported by 5 zones each but live in cli/commands/ or core/ — their physical location implies zone membership they do not have, making every consumer appear as a cross-zone import when these are actually foundation-layer accesses.

Relocate mcp-tools.ts and mcp.ts out of prd-tree-operations into a dedicated transport zone or directory (e.g. src/transport/ or src/mcp/). The zone name 'prd-tree-operations' signals domain logic, not protocol dispatch — co-location actively misleads developers searching for transport code.
Zone "prd-validation" has files across 5 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Dissolve prd-validation as a top-priority action: move epic-correlation.ts and structural.ts into prd-tree-operations (domain utilities); move validate.ts and validate-interactive.ts into the cli zone (CLI commands). This is a zero-logic-change refactor that eliminates the only doubly-failed zone in the codebase.

Zone name 'prd-validation' is a misnomer for its actual contents. Even before dissolving, renaming would prevent future misassignment of CLI commands into this zone based on the misleading 'validation' label.
Zone "PRD Validation" (prd-validation) has critical risk (score: 0.66, cohesion: 0.34, coupling: 0.66) — requires refactoring before new feature development
Zone "recommend-acknowledge" has files across 6 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Zone name 'recommend-acknowledge' omits conflict-detection.ts, which implements a distinct third concern. Consider renaming to 'recommend-workflow' or extracting conflict-detection into a separate zone as it grows — the current name will mislead developers trying to locate conflict logic from zone metadata.

The CLI zone functions as an unintended shared vocabulary layer: 30 reverse imports from unit-core (10), unit-store (7), and unit-analyze (13) all reach upward into CLI for utilities and types. This pattern indicates CLI-layer symbols have been promoted to cross-domain status without deliberate design.

The single concrete reverse import (guided.ts → cli/output.js) is the root cause of the 13-import unit-analyze → cli relationship. Extracting a Logger or OutputPort interface that CLI implements would sever this inversion without restructuring files.

The three hub files (constants.ts, output.ts, tree.ts) are each imported by 5 zones and all three reside in or near prd-tree-operations; these files act as architectural glue that prevents clean zone boundaries — extracting them into a dedicated shared-primitives layer would reduce fan-in and let zone metrics reflect true domain cohesion

Test files in prd-validation (validate.test.ts, validate-interactive.test.ts, etc.) share a community with production files solely because of import proximity; this contaminates cohesion and coupling scores and should be resolved by moving tests to the unit zone or co-locating them with the unit-cli zone rather than letting them drift into a production-dominated zone

recommend-acknowledge is the only multi-file production zone with no reverse imports flowing back into it from its dependencies, making it a clean consumer zone with a well-defined dependency direction.

CLIError defined in src/cli/errors.ts is imported by src/core/move.ts, making a domain module depend on a CLI module. This inverts the expected flow and should be resolved by moving CLIError to a shared errors or foundation layer.

The stark contrast between e2e-tests (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0) and unit-cli (cohesion 0.34) suggests the project's test strategy is bifurcated: end-to-end tests are disciplined and isolated while unit tests have accumulated without a consistent home; a zone-aligned unit test convention (co-locate test files with the zone they cover) would close this gap

unit-analyze participates in a three-zone dependency cycle: unit-core → unit-analyze (22 imports) → unit-store (37 imports) → unit-core (16 imports). This forms a closed loop across three zones and is the most critical structural risk in the codebase.

unit-store → unit-analyze (7 imports) combined with unit-analyze → unit-store (37 imports) forms a second bidirectional loop distinct from the unit-core ↔ unit-store loop; two simultaneous bidirectional cycles in the same zone elevate circular-dependency risk above what either cycle alone would imply

guided.ts owns both the AI clarification protocol (buildClarifyPrompt, clarify) and interactive terminal I/O (promptLine using readline/process.stdin). Embedding process.stdin reading in a domain service makes unit testing impossible without mocking node internals and violates single responsibility — the prompting concern belongs in the CLI command that calls guided.ts.

Schema primitives (v1.ts, levels.ts, validate.ts) and remote sync adapters (sync-engine.ts, adapter-registry.ts, integration-schemas/) have mismatched change rates but share a zone. Schema changes should not trigger re-review of adapter code and vice versa — split into a schema-definitions sub-zone and a sync-adapters sub-zone.

21 public entry points with no enforcing barrel export means the zone has no defined public API — all 64 files are implicitly importable. This is the primary mechanism through which the 98-import unit-core→unit-store coupling accumulates: consumers import whatever internal file is convenient rather than a declared interface.

mcp-tools.ts and mcp.ts are transport-layer files living inside the largest domain zone. A protocol change (e.g. MCP spec update) will require touching domain files, and a domain refactor risks breaking MCP serialization. Extract MCP dispatch to a dedicated transport zone.

prd-validation contains 4 production files that belong to two different architectural layers: epic-correlation.ts and structural.ts are domain utilities (should join prd-tree-operations) while validate.ts and validate-interactive.ts are CLI commands (should join cli). The zone exists as an artifact of import proximity, not architectural intent.

create-from-recommendations.ts re-exports EnrichedRecommendation, RecommendationMeta, and ConflictStrategy from ./types.js solely for backwards compatibility. The project's own CLAUDE.md forbids backwards-compatibility shims; these re-exports should be removed and callers updated to import from the canonical source.

Add a dependency-cruiser or eslint-plugin-import rule that forbids e2e test files from importing any path outside tests/e2e/ or node built-ins. The current perfect isolation (coupling 0) is convention-only and will silently degrade as the test suite grows.

Add e2e tests for the 'health' and 'fix' CLI commands. These commands have production files in prd-tree-operations and prd-store-sync respectively but no corresponding e2e coverage visible in the zone, leaving two non-trivial user-facing flows unprotected at the integration boundary.

Root-level context.md and llms.txt shadow the canonical .sourcevision/CONTEXT.md and .sourcevision/llms.txt. If the root copies are not auto-generated by the build pipeline, they will silently go stale. Either delete them (and update any AI tool configuration pointing at the root path) or add a CI check that verifies the root copies are symlinks or exact copies of the generated sourcevision output.
Zone "Project Root" (project-root) has at-risk risk (score: 0.50, cohesion: 0.00, coupling: 0.00) — approaching architectural risk thresholds
God function: cmdStatus in src/cli/commands/status.ts calls 36 unique functions — consider decomposing into smaller, focused functions
Tightly coupled modules: src/cli/commands/prune.ts and src/cli/output.ts — 60 cross-file calls (unidirectional). Consider extracting shared interface or merging
Zone "Rex Runtime State" (rex-runtime-state) has at-risk risk (score: 0.50, cohesion: 0.00, coupling: 0.00) — approaching architectural risk thresholds
